structured_choices.data_source: ECG waveform dataset from hospital cardiology department: 10,000 patients over 5 years. Raw ECG signals (12-lead, sampled at standard clinical frequency ~250–500 Hz) paired with patient demographics, clinical history (medication use, prior diagnoses, risk factors), and binary outcome labels (cardiac event within follow-up period, defined as hospitalization for myocardial infarction, unstable angina, or coronary revascularization within 12 months post-recording).
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary outcome: cardiac event = any occurrence of acute myocardial infarction, unstable angina requiring hospitalization, or coronary revascularization (PCI/CABG) within 12 months following the index ECG recording. Non-events = no such occurrence within the 12-month follow-up window. Outcomes validated against hospital discharge diagnoses and procedure records.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal + stratified split. Chronologically partition patients by ECG recording date: training cohort (years 1–3, ~6,000 patients), validation cohort (year 4, ~2,000 patients), test cohort (year 5, ~2,000 patients). Within each cohort, stratify by outcome prevalence (event rate) to ensure balanced class representation in train/val/test. Rationale: temporal split prevents data leakage from future information into training; stratification preserves event prevalence across splits and ensures stable metric estimation given the binary classification task. No patient appears in multiple splits.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: ECG preprocessing (filtering, normalization, artifact removal) is defined on training data only and applied identically to validation and test sets. Specifically: (1) Bandpass filtering (0.5–100 Hz) parameters fit on training ECG statistics; (2) Z-score normalization (per-lead mean and standard deviation) computed from training set, applied to val/test; (3) Padding/truncation to fixed length (e.g., 5 seconds of signal at 250 Hz = 1,250 samples per lead) determined by training data distribution; (4) QRS detection and lead alignment performed using fixed algorithm (not data-adaptive). All preprocessing code and parameters versioned. Clinical feature engineering (e.g., age binning, categorical encoding of medication classes) fit on training set only, applied consistently to val/test.
structured_choices.features: Two feature groups for comparison. CNN pathway: raw 12-lead ECG waveforms after preprocessing (standardized length, bandpass filtered, normalized per-lead). Logistic regression pathway: hand-crafted clinical features including age, sex, BMI, smoking status, diabetes status, prior MI, prior revascularization, current medications (beta-blockers, ACE inhibitors, statins—binary indicators), family history, and resting heart rate. No ECG-derived features (e.g., QT interval, ST elevation) are engineered for LR to avoid double-counting; the comparison is waveform (CNN) vs. non-waveform clinical data (LR). Both pathways receive identical train/val/test splits.
structured_choices.model: CNN architecture: 2D convolutional layers (12 input channels, one per ECG lead) with batch normalization, ReLU activations, max-pooling for downsampling, followed by fully connected layers, dropout (p=0.5) for regularization, and a sigmoid output for binary classification. Architecture selected based on domain precedent for ECG signal processing and moderate dataset size. Hyperparameters (filter sizes, layer depths, learning rate, dropout rate, batch size) tuned using validation set performance (see model_selection_data). Training uses binary cross-entropy loss with class weighting to account for potential outcome imbalance.
structured_choices.baseline: Logistic regression trained on clinical features only (demographics + medical history, no waveform data). Both LR and CNN receive equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort: LR hyperparameters (regularization strength L2, class weight balancing, solver) tuned via grid search on the validation set; CNN tuned via randomized search on the same validation set, same metric. LR is fit on the same training cohort, using the same train/val split and evaluation metric. Baseline is fair because: (1) both models see the same data distribution; (2) both are tuned on the same validation set; (3) LR uses established, domain-relevant features (clinical predictors known to associate with cardiac risk). The comparison directly tests whether raw ECG waveform analysis (CNN) outperforms clinical feature-based prediction (LR).
structured_choices.primary_metric: Area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUROC / AUC). Rationale: (1) Clinical decision-making for preventive intervention requires ranking patients by risk; AUC measures discriminative ability across all decision thresholds, independent of threshold choice; (2) Given potential class imbalance (cardiac events often ~10–15% prevalence in observational cohorts), AUC is more robust than accuracy; (3) AUC directly reflects whether the model can separate high-risk from low-risk patients, the operational objective stated in success_metric_context. The model with higher AUC on the held-out test set is declared superior.
secondary_metrics: Sensitivity (recall) at the operating point selected for clinical deployment (e.g., threshold chosen to flag top 20% of patients for intervention).
Specificity at the same operating point (to quantify false-positive burden on resources).
Precision-recall AUC (AUPRC), especially if outcome prevalence is <15%, to guard against inflated AUC in imbalanced settings.

Calibration: Brier score and calibration plots (predicted probability vs. observed frequency in deciles), to assess whether predicted risk scores align with true event rates—critical for clinical adoption.
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Validation cohort (year 4 data, ~2,000 patients) is used for all hyperparameter tuning and model selection. Specifically, for each candidate hyperparameter set, the model is trained on the training cohort and evaluated on the validation cohort using the primary metric (AUROC). The hyperparameter set maximizing validation AUROC is selected. The test cohort (year 5 data, ~2,000 patients) is held completely untouched during this process and used only once at the end for final performance reporting.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set is locked and accessed exactly once. After all hyperparameter tuning and model selection are complete on validation data, the final CNN and baseline LR models are applied to the test set. Test predictions and corresponding metrics (AUROC, sensitivity, specificity, calibration) are reported. No further tuning, threshold adjustment, or model selection is performed using test data. Test results are reported with 95% confidence intervals (via bootstrap or stratified cross-validation on test predictions) to quantify uncertainty.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Medication adherence and lifestyle factors (e.g., recent smoking, diet, exercise) are acknowledged confounds but not directly measured in the available data. Controls: (1) Stratified split ensures both models face identical patient populations across train/val/test, so baseline LR and CNN are equally affected by unmeasured confounding; any difference in their performance is not due to cohort drift. (2) Clinical features provided to LR (medication use, BMI, smoking status) capture proxy measures of lifestyle/adherence; CNN sees only waveforms and has no access to these proxies, creating a controlled comparison. (3) Temporal stratification ensures the test set is prospective relative to training, mimicking deployment conditions. (4) Subgroup analysis: secondary evaluation of model performance stratified by medication class (e.g., statin-users vs. non-users, beta-blocker users vs. non-users) to detect whether confounding by medication adherence creates differential model behavior. (5) Sensitivity analysis: compare CNN and LR performance within patients with high vs. low medication adherence (inferred from pharmacy refill data, if available) to test whether unmeasured confounding biases results.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: CNN-Based ECG Analysis vs. Logistic Regression for Cardiac Risk Prediction

### 1. Data Acquisition and Label Definition

The experiment uses 10,000 ECG waveform recordings from the hospital cardiology department, spanning 5 years. Each recording is a 12-lead ECG signal, sampled at the standard clinical frequency (~250–500 Hz). For each patient, we extract demographics (age, sex), clinical history (prior MI, prior revascularization, medication use), risk factors (smoking, diabetes, BMI), and the outcome: whether the patient experienced a cardiac event within 12 months post-recording. A cardiac event is operationalized as hospitalization for acute myocardial infarction, unstable angina requiring intervention, or coronary revascularization (PCI or CABG), validated against discharge diagnoses and procedure records. This 12-month follow-up window ensures outcomes are known and stable before test set evaluation.

### 2. Train-Validation-Test Split Strategy

We partition data chronologically by ECG recording date to prevent data leakage from future information:
- **Training cohort:** Years 1–3 (~6,000 patients)
- **Validation cohort:** Year 4 (~2,000 patients)
- **Test cohort:** Year 5 (~2,000 patients)

Within each cohort, we perform stratified sampling to maintain the event prevalence (e.g., if overall cardiac event rate is 12%, each split contains ~12% events). This temporal + stratified approach ensures: (a) no patient appears in multiple splits; (b) class balance is preserved across splits; (c) the test set is prospective relative to training, reflecting deployment conditions; and (d) no leakage of future information into model development.

### 3. Preprocessing and Feature Engineering

**ECG preprocessing** (fit on training data, applied to val/test):
- Bandpass filtering (0.5–100 Hz) to remove noise and drift
- Z-score normalization per lead (using training set mean and SD)
- Standardized length: pad or truncate to 5 seconds (1,250 samples at 250 Hz)
- QRS detection and lead alignment using a fixed, non-adaptive algorithm

All preprocessing parameters and code are versioned and applied identically to validation and test sets.

**Feature engineering for Logistic Regression:**
Clinical features only: age, sex, BMI, smoking status, diabetes status, prior MI, prior revascularization, current medications (beta-blockers, ACE inhibitors, statins—binary indicators), family history, resting heart rate. Categorical variables are encoded; numerical features are standardized using training set statistics. No ECG-derived features (e.g., QT interval, ST elevation) are engineered for LR, ensuring the comparison measures raw waveform analysis (CNN) against non-waveform clinical data (LR).

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Primary model (CNN):** A 2D convolutional neural network with 12 input channels (one per ECG lead), batch normalization, ReLU activations, max-pooling, fully connected layers, dropout (p=0.5), and sigmoid output for binary classification. Architecture follows domain precedent for ECG signal processing.

**Baseline (Logistic Regression):** Standard LR trained on clinical features only, with L2 regularization and class weight balancing to account for outcome imbalance.

**Tuning effort:** Both models receive equivalent hyperparameter tuning. CNN hyperparameters (filter sizes, layer depths, learning rate, dropout, batch size) are tuned via randomized search on the validation set. LR hyperparameters (regularization strength, class weights) are tuned via grid search on the same validation set. Both use the primary metric (AUROC) for selection. This ensures fair comparison: the baseline is not handicapped, and any performance gap reflects the value of waveform analysis, not tuning disparity.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric: AUROC (Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve).** Rationale: Clinical decision-making for preventive intervention requires ranking patients by risk; AUROC measures discriminative ability across all thresholds, independent of threshold choice. AUROC is robust to class imbalance and directly reflects the ability to separate high-risk from low-risk patients—the operational objective.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Sensitivity (recall) and specificity at a clinically relevant operating point (e.g., threshold flagging the top 20% of patients for intervention)
- Precision-recall AUC, to guard against inflated AUROC if outcome prevalence is low
- Calibration: Brier score and calibration plots (predicted probability vs. observed frequency in deciles), to ensure predicted risks align with true event rates—essential for clinical adoption

### 6. Model Selection and Validation Protocol

All hyperparameter tuning is performed on the validation cohort (year 4, ~2,000 patients). For each candidate hyperparameter set, the model is trained on the training cohort and evaluated on the validation cohort. The hyperparameter set maximizing validation AUROC is selected. **The test cohort is held completely untouched until the end.** After final model selection, both the CNN and baseline LR are applied once to the test set, and all metrics are reported with 95% confidence intervals (via bootstrap).

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

Unmeasured confounds (e.g., medication adherence, recent lifestyle factors) are acknowledged but cannot be directly controlled in observational data. The design accounts for confounding through: (1) identical temporal + stratified splits for both models, ensuring both face the same cohort composition; (2) clinical features provided to LR (medication use, BMI, smoking status) serve as proxies for adherence/lifestyle, whereas CNN sees only waveforms—this controlled comparison tests whether raw ECG data improves prediction beyond these proxies; (3) subgroup analysis by medication class to detect differential confounding; and (4) sensitivity analysis stratifying by inferred medication adherence (pharmacy refill data). 

**Scope:** This experiment establishes whether CNN-based waveform analysis predicts 12-month cardiac events better than clinical feature-based LR in this cohort. It does not establish causality, generalizability beyond this hospital system, or real-world clinical utility (which requires deployment studies and cost-benefit analysis).
